elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we obtain the reverse Bakry-\'Emery type estimates for a class of hypoelliptic diffusion operator by coupling method.
elements.sent-2411-002: The (right and reverse) Poincar\'e inequalities and the (right and reverse) logarithmic Sobolev inequalities are presented as consequences of such estimates.
elements.sent-2411-003: Wang-Harnack inequality, Hamilton's gradient estimate and Liouville property are also presented by reverse logarithmic Sobolev inequality.
elements.sent-2411-004: We consider the second order differential operator (Kolmogorov type operator) \begin{equation}\label{op1} \mathcal{L}=\frac12div(AD)+\langle x,BD\rangle, x\in \mathbb{R}^{N}\end{equation} where $D=\left(\partial_{x_1 ,\cdots,\partial_{x_N}\right)$, $div$ and $\langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle$ denote, respectively, the gradient, the divergence and the inner product in $\mathbb{R ^N$.}}
elements.sent-2411-005: The matrix $A=(a_{ij )$ and $B=(b_{ij )$ are $N\times N$ real constant matrices in the form \begin{equation}\label{ABM} A=\left(\begin{array}{cc} A_0 0 0 0 \end{array}\right) \ \ and\ \ B=\left( \begin{array}{ccccc} 0 B_1 0 \cdots 0 0 0 B_2 \cdots 0 \vdots \vdots \vdots \ddots \vdots 0 0 0 \cdots B_r 0 0 0 \cdots 0 \end{array} \right)\end{equation} where $A_0$ is a $m_0\times m_0$ symmetric and positive semidefinite and $B_k$ is a $m_{k-1}\times m_k$ matrix of rank $m_k, k=1,2,\ldots,r$, with \begin{equation}m_0\ge m_1\ge \cdots\ge m_r\ge1\ \ \ and\ \ \ \sum_{k=0}^rm_k=N.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{ABM} A=\left({cc} A_0 0 0 0 \right) \ \ and\ \ B=\left( {ccccc} 0 B_1 0 \cdots 0 0 0 B_2 \cdots 0 \vdots \vdots \vdots \ddots \vdots 0 0 0 \cdots B_r 0 0 0 \cdots 0  \right)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-006: Hypothesis \eqref{ABM implies that the operator $L$ verifies the classical H\"ormander condition \cite{H67 .}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Define, for every $t\in \mathbb{R}$, $E(t)=\exp(-tB^*)$ ($B^*$ is the transpose matrix of $B$) and \begin{equation}\label{C-M} \ \mathcal{C}(t):=\int_0^t E(s)AE^*(s)ds\end{equation} Under the assumption \eqref{ABM , we have $\mathcal{C (t)>0$ for every $t>0$, it means that $\mathcal{C (t)>0$ is positive definite, see \cite{LP94 , \cite{LPP02 etc. By H\"ormander's theorem \cite{H67 , the operator $\mathcal{L}$ defined in \eqref{op1 is hypoelliptic and generators a Markov process $X_t$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: The transition probability density of the processs $X_t$ is Gaussian with respect to the Lebesgue measure $dx$, see e.g {\cite[Page 148]{H67 , {\cite[Theorem 1 and Theorem 4]{K72 or {\cite[Page 30]{LP94 for explicit expression of the transition probability: \begin{equation}\label{fs} h(x,t;\xi,\tau)=p(x-E(t-\tau)\xi,t-\tau)\end{equation} $0\le \tau<t$, where $p(x,t;0,0)=0$ if $t\le0$ and \begin{equation}p(x,t)=\frac{(2\pi)^{-\frac{N}{2}}}{\sqrt{\det C(t)}}\exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}\langle C^{-1}(t)x,x\rangle\right), \ t>0.\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: We can express the Markov process $X_t=(X_1(t),\cdots, X_{r+1 (t))$ by the following stochastic differential equation \begin{equation}\label{markov}\begin{cases} dX_1(t) =\sigma dB(t), dX_{2}(t) =B_1^*X_1(t)dt \vdots dX_{r+1}(t) =B_r^*X_r(t)dt \end{cases}\end{equation} where $\sigma$ is a $m_0\times m_0$ constant matrix satisfying $\sigma\sigma^*=A_0$, for $1\le i\le r$, $B(t)$ is a Brownian motion in $\mathbb{R ^{m_0}$ and $X_{i+1 (t)$ is a $\mathbb{R ^{m_i -$valued process ($1\le i\le r$).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: In particular, $r=1$ and $A_0=B_1=1$, \eqref{op1 reduces to the Kolmogorov operator initially introduced by A.. N.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Kolmogorov in \cite{Ko34 , which is the simplest example of a hypoelliptic seconde order differential operator, see \cite{H67 .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In the case of $r=1$ and $A_0=B_1=id$, Baudoin et al \cite{BGM20 and the very recent paper \cite{BGHKM23 obtained gradient bounds and other functional inequalities including reverse Poincar\'e inequality, reverse logarithmic Sobolev inequality by generalized $\Gamma-$calculus, see \cite{BE85,BGL14 for Bakry-\'Emery $\Gamma-$calculus in Riemannian manifolds.}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The authors \cite{QZ1 generalized the results to the iterated Kolmogorov diffusion operator ($A_0=B_1=\cdots=B_r=id \ (r\ge1)$) by a similar modified $\Gamma-$calculus.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The main observation of \cite{QZ1 is the reverse Bakry-\'Emery type inequalities, which generates the reverse Poincar\'e inequality and reverse logarithmic Sobolev inequality.}
elements.sent-2411-015: For non-Gaussian degenerate diffusions, Baudoin et el.

elements.sent-2411-017: On the other side, the Li-Yau gradient estimates for the positive solutions of \begin{equation}\label{heat}\mathcal{L}u=\partial_tu\end{equation} has been proved by Pascucci and Polidoro \cite{PP04 by the original variational argument due to Li and Yau \cite{LY86 , see also \cite{GL90 , \cite{LP94 and references therein.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The Li-Yau type gradient estimate can give the parabolic Harnack inequality, the latter can compare the solution (heat) of \eqref{heat of increasing time at two different points.. R.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Hamilton \cite{Ham11 obtained the matrix differential Harnack estimate for hypoelliptic heat equation \eqref{heat in the case of $r=1,A_0=B_1=1$ by using Riccati type equations.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Huang \cite{HH14 extended Hamilton's result to slightly general model and conjectured that the matrix differential Harnack estimate holds for hypoelliptic operator \eqref{op1 with structure \eqref{ABM .}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: This conjecture has been solved by the authors in \cite{QZ2 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Meanwhile, there is a different gradient estimate, discovered by R.. S.
elements.sent-2411-023: Hamilton in \cite{Ham93 , for bounded positive solutions of $Lu=\partial_tu$ on compact Riemannian manifolds under the lower bounded of the Ricci curvature.}
elements.sent-2411-024: This type of gradient estimate (called Hamilton's elliptic gradient estimate) can give the Harnack inequality with power (see for example Corollary \ref{harn-ham3 ), which can compare the heat of the same time at two different points.}
elements.sent-2411-025: One may ask whether Hamilton's elliptic gradient estimate hold for the hypoelliptic heat equation \eqref{heat .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Since the Ricci curvature (or Bakry-\'Emery $\Gamma_2$ curvature) of hypoelliptic operator like $\mathcal{L}$ is $-\infty$ every where, the original method in \cite{Ham93 doesn't work.}
elements.sent-2411-027: It is the start point of this paper to derive the Hamilton's elliptic gradient estimate for the hypoelliptic heat equation \eqref{heat .}
elements.sent-2411-028: To this end, we intend to study the gradient bounds and funtional inequalities for the hypoelliptic diffusion operator \eqref{markov .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Since the lower bound of the associated Bakry \'Emery $\Gamma_2$ curvature of $\mathcal{L}$ with structure \eqref{ABM is unclear (see the computations of Kolmogorov diffusion operator and iterated Kolmogorov diffusion operator in \cite{BGM20,QZ1 ), new method is needed.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: For special choices of $A$ and $B_1$, synchronous coupling was introduced in \cite[Proposition 2.10]{BGM20 to prove the $L^q$ version of the right Bakry-\'Emery type estimate.}
elements.sent-2411-031: It is said that it seems difficult to prove the reverse Poincar\'e and the reverse logarithmic Sobolev inequalities for the semigroup generated by Kolmogorov diffusion by using coupling techniques, see Remark 2.11 in \cite{BGM20 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Here we succeed in applying coupling techniques to derive the reverse Bakry-\'Emery type estimates, which imply reverse Poincar\'e and the reverse logarithmic Sobolev inequalities, see Corollary \ref{BE2-cor or Corollary \ref{BE2-cor-3 , and Corollary \ref{BEln-cor-3 , which is the main contribution of this paper.}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-034: To illustrate the coupling method we begin with the simple case $r=1$ in Section \ref{sec-2.1 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The Bakry-\'Emery type estimates with free parameters are presented, see Proposition \ref{prop-BE ; Different choices of the free parameter gives right and reverse Bakry-\'Emery type estimates (Corollary \ref{BE2-cor ).}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Then we extend to the general case $r\ge1$ in section \ref{sec-2.2 , see Proposition \ref{prop-BE-3 , Proposition \ref{prop-BEln-3 and Corollary \ref{BE2-cor-3 , Corollary \ref{BEln-cor-3 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: In section \ref{sec-3 , we give various applications of Bakry-Emery type inequalities, see Theorem \ref{PI-3 for the (right and reverse) Poincar\'e inequality, and Theorem \ref{thm-LSI-3 for the (right and reverse) logarithmic Sobolev inequality.}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Wang-Harnack inequality, Hamilton's elliptic gradient estimate and Liouville property are also presented as consequences of reverse logarithmic Sobolev inequality.
elements.sent-2411-039: Throughout this paper, we use the following {\bf Notations: For any vector $x=(x_1,\cdots,x_d)^*,y=(y_1,\cdots,y_d)^*\in \mathbb{R ^{d}$, $q\ge 1$, we denote $\|x\|_q=\left(\sum_{i=1 ^{d |x_i|^q\right)^{\frac1q ,\ \|x\|_{A,q =\|\sigma x\|_q$ and the inner product $\langle x,y\rangle=\sum_{i=1 ^dx_iy_i$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: We use the notation $\partial_jf=\partial_{x_j f, 1\le j\le d$ for $f\in C^1\left(\mathbb{R ^d\right)$.}}

elements.sent-2411-130: We also have the following Hamilton's elliptic gradient estimate by the reverse logarithmic Sobolev inequality.
elements.sent-2411-131: Let $u$ be the positive bounded solution to the hypoelliptic heat equation $\mathcal{L u(x,t)=\partial_tu(x,t)$ with the initial value $0\le f\le C$ ($C$ is a constant), we have for any $x\in\mathbb{R ^{m_0}\times\cdots \times\mathbb{R ^{m_r}$ and $t>0$, \begin{equation}\label{Ham-eq3} \frac12\langle \mathcal{C}(t)\nabla \ln u,\nabla \ln u\rangle\le \ln \frac{C}{u}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Then by the reverse logarithmic Sobolev inequality \eqref{RLSI-0-3 , \begin{equation}\frac12u\langle C(t)\nabla \ln u,\nabla \ln u\rangle\le u\ln(C)-u\ln u.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-134: Under the assumption of Proposition \ref{Ham3 , we have for $t>0$, $\alpha>1$ and any $x,y\in\mathbb{R ^{m_0}\times\cdots\times \mathbb{R ^{m_r}$, \begin{equation}u^{\alpha}(x,t)\le u(y,t)C^{\alpha-1}e^{\frac{\alpha}{2(\alpha-1)}\langle C^{-1}(t) (y-x),y-x \rangle}.\end{equation}.}}}

elements.sent-2411-137: Thus for any $\alpha>1$, applying the elementary inequality $(a+b)^2\le \alpha a^2+\frac{\alpha {\alpha-1 b^2$ for $a,b\in \mathbb{R}$, \begin{equation}\ln\frac{C}{u}(y,t) \le \left(\sqrt{\ln\frac{C}{u}(x,t)}+\frac{\sqrt{2}}2 \sqrt{\langle C^{-1} (y-x),y-x \rangle}\right)^2 \le \alpha \ln\frac{C}{u(x,t)}+\frac{\alpha}{2(\alpha-1)}\langle C^{-1} (y-x),y-x \rangle.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-138: As a consequence of Proposition \ref{Ham3 , the following Liouville property holds.}
